speaker: interviewer
text: Fifth of November 1995, and we are in Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leon, please, can you introduce us to this lady?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Mimi. We've been married for 34 years, actually last weekend. And this is Mimi, ex Mimi Goldman. And 34 years we've been together. And it's been a very happy life together. And I hope it stays like that until 120.

speaker: interviewee
text: Amen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please, God.

speaker: interviewee
text: We'll be 119. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewee
text: He's always joking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mimi, we heard the most amazing story of how Leon has come through incredibly difficult-- an incredibly difficult childhood, and he's just come out on top. And he told us how you met as young people [INTERPOSING VOICES]. Can you describe how it was when you met him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually I meet him a long time before I actually started to go out with him. People always said to me, you're from France? You must know Leon Wise-- for years; since I was about 13 or 14. And I never had met him. And one day I became involved with Vivi Davidson, who was his guardian. And she never said anything, or I know who she was. And years-- several years later, when it was 1956, he had just come back from overseas. And my best friend invited me to a party where he was. And he was surrounded by so many people, I still didn't meet him. But my best friend married a very close friend. And we eventually became friendly. And in 1960 he asked me out. And it was my birthday, and six months later we were engaged. So, yeah. But it took several years. But the common backgrounds--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was a bit too young for me to go out with. She caught up, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: But our common background really was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Much the same.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much the same. All of the experiences and things like that that brought us very much together. A lot of similarities in our thinking and background.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think in life it's most important. You have to look for your own background. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was good looking, and he had hair.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: A cute French accent. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Wavy hair, no? Good-bye.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that today was a very special day for you. It was an anniversary, as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's a very-- it's a coincidence actually. I arrived on the fifth of November, 1948 in Australia, and my daughter had an anniversary-- wedding anniversary--

speaker: interviewee
text: Her sixth wedding anniversary is today.

speaker: interviewee
text: --is today. Ilana. Ilana Kaplan. So it has a lot of meaning to us, you know, the 5th of November. And of course, don't want to mention actually what happened today. On the radio we just heard the bad news about Mr. Raven passing away. So there will even be another Thing to remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think for the Jewish community throughout the world, a tremendous blow for peace and maybe [INAUDIBLE]. It's hard for everybody, I think. Today is a hard time to make an interview with that in our background.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what else can we say? We have wonderful children. So, I'm sure Leon told you about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yes. Leon is there is something you'd like to say? Is there any special message that you would like to give them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would like to convey to my children to be honest, and not to forget their heritage. And I must say, that I cannot complain. Because they are wonderful children. And these-- you know-- although it's difficult to bring up children in this day and age, well, I've got nothing to complain about. They are wonderful children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're going to see pictures of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they married fine, young people. And they have given us some wonderful grandchildren that we look forward to, I think, having lots of fun with in many years to come.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Leon. Thank you, Mimi. Thank you very much indeed.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's been a pleasure. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I hope people will learn from our past experience how important it is to stick together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to have peace in the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So little children don't suffer. And parents' children don't lose their parents, and parents don't see the suffering of their children. Yes, please God. Thank you both very much, indeed. Thank you. We're going to have some pictures now. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And cut.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are these people, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of my parents. I believe it's a photograph when they were married in Poland in 1926 or '27. And that photograph I photographed up after the war, where we lived and gave the original photo frame to my sister, being the eldest one of the three. And this, my mother, this [? Sybil ?] [? Scott. ?] And my father Simon Wajselbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city in Poland were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came from, I believe, from Sokolov. And she's from Warsaw, Warschau. And so this is a photograph to which I took from the original one. My sister has the original one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and cut.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother. And When she lived in Paris, just before we left her, this is how she looked like. And I found this, again, after the war in-- when we went to see, like I told you in the story, in Paris, where we lived. It was in the sewing machine, in the drawer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name and the address.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ides. Ides. And we lived in [SPEAKING FRENCH] in Paris [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph, again, I found in a drawer of a-- after the war, in my mother's sewing machine. And the line in the center, it was just folded. So we put it-- put it together. And his name is Simon Wajselbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: When do you think this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would imagine that was taken in France, because they had these automatic-- Wajselbaum you know, you go into a booth, and you put some money in. In French we call it [SPEAKING FRENCH]. And that's what-- it was definitely taken in Paris before the war. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken and smuggled out from a camp. My father was taken from Paris to Pithiviers, was in at a camp with the German or the French set up in the south of France. And you can see him there with the hat on the right-hand side, right in the back. It was smuggled out of that camp, and sent to my mother with a letter in Yiddish. Unfortunately, I cannot find that letter. I've got it here somewhere. And it was amazed-- people are amazed that photograph had been smuggled out of the camp and sent to us. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother was where?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was in Drancy. Drancy was, again, another camp. It was a-- a transit camp to go to Auschwitz. And it became full. They moved X amount of Jews to Auschwitz and then new ones came in. My father followed suit afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is, again, in the camp Pithiviers, where my father was taken. He is in the front, in-- on the left-hand side next to the gentleman with the cigarette in mouth. My father wears a hat, and he's got the overcoat. His name is Simon Wajselbaum. And there again, he was-- that photograph was smuggled out, I think it was in 1942, to my mother. And it made its way to us in the childrens' home in Brout-Vernet. I just don't know how we ever got it. But this is the original photograph.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken in 1941, '42, I think. And that was in Brout-Vernet during the war in not far from Chateau des Morrelles.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in one of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? In ?] home, yes, in Chateau des Morrelles in Brout-Vernet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's just my brother and myself. My brother. Henry, and myself at the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your full names, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon At the time I was known as Leon Cybulska.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother Henry Cybulska

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. This is a few kids. We were-- I'm right in the front, my brother at the back, with one of our teacher, Mademoiselle [? Cohen. ?] Unfortunately she died in 1942. And she was a wonderful lady, by the name of-- the name of Mademoiselle [? Cohen. ?] And there is my brother right in the center in the back. And I'm on the side here on the right-hand side. But we was in Chateau des Morelles in Brout-Vernet. And each of our, because of the washing, we were given numbers. My numbers were 382. My brother was 30-- 381. And my sister, was three-- if I remember rightly, she had 379. Yes. That was our number-- not on the arm but on the clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're wearing the white beret?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm wearing the white beret. Yes. And my brother's right at the back there with Mademoiselle [? Cohen. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And he's Henry Cybulska?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Henry and Leon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cybulska.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cybulska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. This is again taken in Brout-Vernet in 1941 IN [SPEAKING FRENCH] in Chateau des Morelles. And you can see us with-- with our group. My brother has got a white shirt with a-- in the right in the center. And I'm on his right squinting, and my head is a sideways a bit. Henry Cybulska and Leon Wajselbaum, and Mademoiselle [? Cohen ?]. That was in Brout-Vernet in-- in the-- in the garden there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're right on the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm second from the left. Looking at it, second on the left. Just squinting my eyes. And actually I'm almost dressed up the same as my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's there in the center. Right in the center.

speaker: interviewer
text: The taller boy with the watch on?

speaker: interviewee
text: The taller one, yes, yes. With the white shirt and the hat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the first photograph taken just when we arrived at the Chateau des Morelles in Brout-Vernet, maison [SPEAKING FRENCH]. You can see me right in front with the little white beret. I'm standing-- No, actually-- yes-- [INAUDIBLE] with the white beret. That's right. I was, at the time, I think 6, 6 and 1/2 years old. And that was taken between '39 and 1940 in Vichy. That's the first time we went in. And you can see Miriam Dybnis and [? Mr. Vail, ?] and Leon Cybulska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. This photograph was taken, again, in Chateau des Morelles in Brout-Vernet during the war-- 1941, '42. This is Rabbi Weikselbaum. No relation to me. He was-- we were teaching Gemora. And he's the father of Miriam Dybnis. And I'm standing there in front with a hand on my hip.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was a wonderful rabbi-- Rabbi Weikselbaum. We used to know him before the war with Rabbi [? Shnasum ?] at the [? Haida ?]. And that's [? Mr. Vail-- ?] whereabouts is he? No. He's not in that photograph. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. This photograph was taken, again, in Chateau des Morelles. The back row-- the last person there-- his name was Edmond Belk. He's a-- he's a friend of mine who actually was taken by the German and disappeared. He was-- I believe he was shot. Next to him, his name is [? Simon ?] [INAUDIBLE]. I have a story to tell you about this-- this fellow. He's today the dean of the University of La Sorbonne. Edmond Belk, the second last person, we saw him about two years ago. We went to see him in Paris. I had not seen him for 40 years. And we saw him in the hospital. He had a transplant-- a kidney transplant. And when I was told that he was the dean of the faculty of mathematics, I thought to myself, what a-- this-- I couldn't believe that this fellow grew up together and today is the head of a-- the dean of a university-- one of the most prestigious university of the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, in fact, I praised him and I said to him-- he said to me, do you remember such and such? I said, yes. And he said, well, he's the head of the nuclear [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whatever it's called in France. He's the head of the-- and I thought to myself, you know, what a wonderful thing it is, you know, that these people suffered and yet they became today they're the top people in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were little-- they were orphans--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were orphans with us. I said to him, where did you learn mathematics?

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, don't you remember? We had-- [? Mr. Vail-- ?] used to put in-- when we used to go on-- on the excursion-- he used to draw in the sand, you know, a triangle. And he said what is the square of this, and you know. He says he learned mathematics like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we was-- we used to play chess. He was always a champion. He and my brother were the champion of chess play in the home. And today I was amazed that today he is the head of the university.

speaker: interviewer
text: Incredible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is taken in Chateau des Morelles. You can see in the background very faintly-- that's my sister, Raymonde Cybulska, she was known then. She is now Raymonde Feuerwerker My brother, Henry Cybulska. He's known Henry Wise. And myself, Leon Wise, and Leon Cybulska then. And that was taken in 1942 in the home during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the [FRENCH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the [FRENCH] home. Yes. [FRENCH] means [FRENCH]. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph, again, was taken in Chateau des Morelles in Brout-Vernet in 1942, '43 I presume. My brother is on the right-hand side holding his elbow, and in the white shirt. And I'm next to the chap who's got the braces in the dark jumper. And that was taken not long before we had to split. And at the back of us, you can see the teacher, Henri Dybnis, and other teachers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is Henry Dybnis? Henry Dybnis is the one with the beret right behind us in the forefront-- in the middle. Third from the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right in the middle. That's Henri Dybnis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your names, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My name-- my brother's name is Henry Cybulska. Myself, Leon Cybulska at the time. And now we are known as Wise.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is taken in Brout-Vernet again. And my sister is on the second last, right at the back on the right-hand side. Her name, Raymonde Cybulska. Next to her, there is our teacher, is Miriam Dybnis, who looked after us.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's the taller lady?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's a tall lady, right at the back next to my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, of course, there is other friends which are still alive today.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This photograph, again, was taken in Chateau des Morelles Maison [FRENCH] in Brout-Vernet in 1942. And you can see my sister, Raymonde Cybulska. She's the third on the-- looking in front. She's the third on the left-hand side. And sitting right in the middle is, again, Miriam Dybnis. Now I know it's out of sequence, with Miriam Dybnis and Henri Dybnis. When I arrived in Australia-- I'll tell you very quickly-- I was sitting at the Davis's on a Friday night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she had invited some friends by the name of Mr. and Mrs. Ketison. And she was reading-- he was reading a letter about the cousin in Los Angeles. And suddenly-- I couldn't speak English-- but I heard the name Dybnis. So I stopped him, and I said, did you say Dybnis? He said, yes, Miriam and Henri Dybnis.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to him, you know, they were doing the war with me. I said-- he said they're cousins of mine. And I didn't know where they were up to now. And I couldn't believe it. And this is how we made my acquaintance with the Dybnises. I was in tears. I could not believe that I had found them again, that this gentleman, who was invited at a Friday night table, should read a letter to the Davis's about his cousin, Miriam and Henri Dybnis.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, there's only one in the world. Dybnis is not a common name. It must be them. And he showed me-- and I showed him the photograph. And he burst out in tears. Isn't that amazing?

speaker: interviewer
text: Absolutely incredible.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know. And so we see each other. We spent last New Year's Eve together in Los Angeles. We are in touch all the time. And to them we are like a family, of course. And they were so happy to see me in New Year's Eve-- last New Year's Eve-- with Mimi in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wonderful story. Wonderful story.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. This is actually the Chateau des Morelles in Brout-Vernet, [SPEAKING FRENCH]. And that was taken-- if I remember, I presume it was taken-- in 1942. And you can see my sister on the veranda there in the white dress right at the back. And Miriam Dybnis, she's just-- no, not with the white dress. I'm terribly sorry. Miriam Dybnis is right at the back on the left-hand side. And my sister's right in the font in front of her, obliterating her face.

speaker: interviewer
text: And those are the French windows that got opened at night during the winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were sleeping?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in, again, Chateau des Morelles. This is Miriam Dybnis. And I think it was taken not long before we-- we left to go to-- to the home of Rabbi Schnelsohn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Miriam Dybnis was the lady who looked after--

speaker: interviewee
text: She looked after us. Yes. Miriam Dybnis was our-- well, teacher, if you like to sayk you know. She was in command of all of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And round about what time was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was taken early '43, I would imagine, this one here. 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. This photo is Henri Dybnis with his wife, Miriam. And this was taken in a more happy time. It was taken in Switzerland in one of the homes, I would imagine in [? Spica. ?] And see it-- they-- well, they are much older than the other photograph. That's Henri and Miriam Dybnis. They were-- they were looked after us right through the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go. This was taken in [? Spica. ?] As you can see, it was winter. And I am right in front-- the little one. And when we used to go to school. We used to shovel our way to go to school there. And that's in, of course, in a much happier time in Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give us the name of the place and your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name, Leon Cybulska again. And then it was in-- in 1944, that was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the main street of [? Spica ?] in the home where we were. We were going on the way to school there. And that particular day, my sister came to visit me-- Raymonde Cybulska. She's at the back there. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I can't see. I'm on the-- I'm on one of those luge, what you call-- sitting down on the luge. And this you can see how we used to go to school. And that was in 1944 in Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. This is-- this is the family Kogan. They were-- they looked after us in Brout-Vernet near Vichy during the war. They were-- they were in charge of looking for the food. And you can see those two kids. They, unfortunately-- and the father-- was killed during the war, while the mother was waiting for a child. In 1943 she gave birth to a child who lives now in-- in Melbourne. And when he came to see me, he told me about his parents. And I showed him that photograph. He couldn't believe that I had from-- he's never met his brothers and sister. Unfortunately they were killed during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. This is the three of us-- my sister, and my brother, and I. My sister, Raymonde, in the middle-- Cybulska. Henry on the left-hand side-- Henry Cybulska. And Leon, myself, on the-- on the right-hand side. And that was just I would imagine after the war that was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in the Chateau des Morelles. The day we left we split to go to Switzerland. And you can see the Dybnises in the back. And this gentleman on the right-hand side is the chap, Edmond, which was killed, and myself, my brother, and my sister-- Raymonde [INAUDIBLE] Raymonde Cybulska, Henry Cybulska, and Leon Cybulska. Unfortunately Edmond never came back. He was taken by the German. He got lost, and he got taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was myself. It was a-- like a passport for a refugee, for the whoever came to Switzerland during the war. Leon Cybulska, as you can see. And with this we were allowed to have-- to have coupons every now and again.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Switzerland [? Vatvil. ?] That's where the home we were in 19-- end of '43 and '44. And you can see this was like half of-- half of the building was a pension. People used to come-- the people from the village used to come and eat, because it was a restaurant. And we used-- part of the on the left-hand side, that was our children's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in [? Spica ?] in one of the home [SPEAKING FRENCH] again. I'm right in front leaning on my knees. I've got my hand on my knee, Leon Cybulska. And with my good friend, Albert. I forgot his name now, but I'll remember his name. And his first name. And they were a happy time again there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1946 when my sister got married in Geneva. My brother, Henry Cybulska, and myself, Leon Cybulska. And that's taken in Geneva just before we came to-- six months before we came to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Mr. And Mrs. John Davis. I was assigned to go to their place on weekends. And they ended up being like adopted parents to me right up to the present time. I'm on the left-hand side, Leon Wise as known then. And this is Viva and Johnny Davis.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Sydney?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sydney. In Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This one is my daughter, Ilana. She's married to Maurice Kaplan. And so she is known as Maurice Kaplan-- as Ilana-- I'm sorry. I'm getting bamboozled. She's known in-- as Ilana Kaplan. She has three children, three children, and they're gorgeous.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my youngest daughter, Rahel. She's married to Denni Shagri. And they both living in Sydney. And she is delightful. A delightful couple.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go. This is my son with his wife, Michelle. My son Simon-- Simon Wise, and Michelle. Today they are living in London. And they have a little boy-- Little Jordy, we call him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. These are my grandchildren with my wife, Mimi-- Mimi Wise. Now my little grandson, the eldest one is Jonathan Kaplan. He's on the left-hand side. As gorgeous as he is. And in the middle you've got his little brother, Mikey, always laughing. And then on the right hand side, Jordy, which is Simon and Michelle's little boy. And they live in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Leon. Thank you very, very much indeed.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape five of the interview with Leon Wise. It's the 5th of November 1995 and we are in Sydney, Australia. And that's also the end of the whole interview.
